title: pi-behavior-monitors write-action path bypasses block-api and drifts silently from destination block schemas
body:
executeWriteAction at packages/pi-behavior-monitors/index.ts:1316-1372 writes to .project/ blocks via raw fs.readFileSync / fs.writeFileSync, completely bypassing pi-project's block-api.ts (writeBlock, appendToBlock, updateItemInBlock) and schema-validator.ts. The writeCfg.schema field declared in monitor.json action blocks (e.g. 'schemas/issues.schema.json' in fragility.monitor.json and work-quality.monitor.json) is vestigial — read into writeCfg but never passed to any validator. This is a single package-level bug with multiple symptoms:

  (a) Schema drift uncaught at write time. The fragility and work-quality monitors ship with write templates that produce entries missing four required fields (title, body, location, package) and using enum values outside the schema (category: 'fragility', priority: '{severity}' → 'warning'). Evidence: the orphan entry fragility-fragility-mnnut4nc at .project/issues.json[35], produced by the shipped fragility.monitor.json template, which fails AJV validation against .project/schemas/issues.schema.json on all six constraints.

  (b) Vocabulary mismatch between monitor internals and issue tracker. Monitors classify with a severity vocabulary (warning/error/critical) and a monitor-name vocabulary (fragility/work-quality/hedge), but the issue-tracker schema has priority (low/medium/high/critical) and category (primitive/issue/cleanup/capability/composition). Neither vocabulary was aligned. The write template treats the two as interchangeable — they are not.

  (c) Data loss on malformed JSON. index.ts:1335-1340: 'let data = {}; try { data = JSON.parse(...) } catch { /* create fresh */ }'. If the destination file has any parse error (interrupted write, manual edit in progress, filesystem corruption), the catch silently sets data to empty and the subsequent write overwrites ALL prior entries with just the new one. There is no warning, no backup, no refusal. A single interrupted tmp+rename or manual edit can wipe issues.json.

  (d) Read-modify-write race. No inter-process locking between concurrent monitor invocations. Two monitors firing in the same window both read the same state, both append, second write loses the first write's addition.

  (e) No schema resolution. writeCfg.schema is a string like 'schemas/issues.schema.json' — but resolved against what directory? The bundled monitor examples use a relative path that would resolve differently depending on cwd. The code never resolves it at all because it never uses it, so the ambiguity is academic but documents intent that was abandoned.

Canonical resolution: executeWriteAction must route through pi-project's block-api (writeBlock/appendToBlock with name='issues' or similar) so that every write goes through schema-validator.ts with AJV. Write templates in monitor.json specs must produce entries that conform to the destination block's schema — which implies either rewriting the templates in fragility.monitor.json and work-quality.monitor.json to include title/body/location/package and map category/priority to the block's enums, OR extending issues.schema.json category and priority enums to include the monitor-produced values, OR (preferred) routing monitor findings to a scope-owned monitor-findings registry per FGAP-002 (issue-056) instead of the global issues.json. Concurrency via block-api's atomic write path (tmp + rename) with file-level locking. Data-loss protection by refusing to overwrite unparseable destination files — surface the error to the monitor caller instead of silently wiping.

Cross-references: the orphan entry (fragility-fragility-mnnut4nc) is untouched by this issue and remains at .project/issues.json[35] as evidence — deletion or migration is a separate scope decision. The monitor-side fix compounds with issue-062 (provider-pin) and issue-063 (thinking:on dead config) as the three load-bearing pi-behavior-monitors defects discovered this session. All three indicate the package's write and dispatch paths were authored before the current schema discipline landed and have drifted. Canonical owner for the structural fix: FEAT-001 consumer migration arc (.project/features.json) should absorb the executeWriteAction rewrite as a new STORY once the decision-gated work lands, OR the user may scope it as a separate follow-up epic.
